title: Prayer of the Guest Chaplain of the U.S. Senate: Rabbi Jacob Voorsanger on 30 June 1898 (fragment)
author: Aharon N. Varady (transcription)

coauthors.name: Aharon N. Varady (transcription)
coauthors.bio: Aharon Varady (M.A.J.Ed./JTSA Davidson) is a volunteer transcriber for the Open Siddur Project. If you find any mistakes in his transcriptions, please let him know. Shgiyot mi yavin; Ministarot naqeni שְׁגִיאוֹת מִי־יָבִין; מִנִּסְתָּרוֹת נַקֵּנִי "Who can know all one's flaws? From hidden errors, correct me" (Psalms 19:13). If you'd like to directly support his work, please consider donating via his Patreon account. (Varady also translates prayers and contributes his own original work besides serving as the primary shammes of the Open Siddur Project and its website, opensiddur.org.)

coauthors.name: Jacob Voorsanger
coauthors.bio: Rabbi Jacob Voorsanger (1852-1908), born in Amsterdam, was a prominent Reform movement rabbi and scholar in the United States. He was educated at the Jewish Theological Seminary of Amsterdam, and received the degree of D.D. from Hebrew Union College in Cincinnati. He officiated as rabbi at Philadelphia (1873-76), Washington, DC (1876-77), Providence, Rhode Island (1877-78), Houston, Texas (1878-86), and Sand Francisco, California (1886-1908). From 1881 to 1883 Voorsanger was editor of The Jewish South (Houston), and from 1883 to 1886 of the Sabbath Visitor (Cincinnati). Starting 1886, he began serving as rabbi at the Temple Emanu-El, San Francisco, California. In 1894 he was appointed professor of Semitic languages and literature at the University of California. He also officiated as chaplain and special lecturer at the Leland Stanford, Jr., University. In 1895 he founded the paper Emanu-El.

excerpt: This is a fragment of a prayer offered before the United States Senate on 30 June 1898 by Rabbi Jacob Voorsanger, rabbi of Temple Emanuel and professor of Semitic Languages at the University of California, San Francisco.
content:
Contribute a translation Source (English)
[…]
all elements which may come
from these sacred walls
be extended
in the form of God’s blessing
to all humanity,
and that the flag under which we live,
that for which our fathers fought,
be forever the sacred emblem of liberty
until time shall be no more.
[…]

This is a fragment of a prayer offered before the United States Senate on 30 June 1898 by Rabbi Jacob Voorsanger, rabbi of Temple Emanuel and professor of Semitic Languages at the University of California, San Francisco. Before 1904, prayers offered by guest chaplains were rarely recorded. Full records of these prayers in both Jewish newspapers and newspaper for a wide audience is spotty. Hopefully, a full record of the entire prayer by Rabbi Voorsanger survives… somewhere. If you have seen a more complete record of the prayer, please leave a comment or contact us.

Source(s)

Rabbi Jacob Voorsanger’s Prayer before the US Senate (NY Times, 1 July 1898), p. 4
